author: Anonymous Author
status: LOCKED — deviations must be labeled EXPLORATORY
primary_rq: Does trust in AI output accuracy (AIAcc) moderate the negative relationship between perceived AI job threat (AIThreat) and developer job satisfaction (JobSat)? Specifically: is the threat-satisfaction link stronger among developers who highly trust AI accuracy — because high trust makes the threat more credible?
theoretical_basis: Cognitive threat appraisal theory (Lazarus & Folkman 1984) distinguishes credible from non-credible threats. A job threat perceived as credible — i.e., backed by trust in AI capability — should produce stronger negative affect and lower job satisfaction than a threat deemed unlikely to materialize. Developers who both feel threatened AND trust AI accuracy face a 'double-bind': they believe AI can do their job and worry it will replace them. Those who distrust AI accuracy can rationalize the threat away. Therefore, AIAcc should amplify (moderate) the AIThreat → JobSat pathway.
h0: The negative effect of AIThreat on JobSat does not differ significantly across levels of AIAcc (interaction coefficient β_interaction = 0).
h1: Among developers who highly trust AI accuracy (AIAcc = Highly trust), the negative relationship between AIThreat (Yes) and JobSat is significantly stronger (more negative) than among those who distrust AI accuracy (AIAcc = Highly distrust / Somewhat distrust). Formally: β_AIThreat×AIAcc < 0 (the interaction amplifies the threat effect for high-trust respondents).
expected_direction: Negative moderation: higher AIAcc trust → stronger negative association between AIThreat and JobSat
novelty_vs_v1_reason: v1 tested AIThreat as a main-effect predictor of JobSat vs salary. v3 tests an interaction (moderation by AIAcc), which is structurally distinct — it asks not 'does AIThreat matter' but 'when does AIThreat matter most'. Different RQ structure, different statistical test.
novelty_vs_v2_reason: v2 compared AIThreat vs AISelect as competing predictors. v3 still uses AIThreat but asks a conditional question (moderated by AIAcc). Overlap in focal variable (AIThreat), but the research question, design, and expected mechanism are distinct.
novelty_score_justification: v3 introduces moderation (interaction) as the primary analytical frame — not previously tested in v1/v2. AIAcc as a theoretically-motivated moderator is novel. Score is MEDIUM (not LOW) because AIThreat remains a focal variable shared with v1/v2. No redesign required; MEDIUM overlap does not trigger rejection per protocol.

mediation_hypothesis.mediator: Frustration (operationalized as binary: has any listed frustration = 1, 'None of these' = 0)
mediation_hypothesis.rationale: Perceived AI job threat activates occupational uncertainty, which manifests as heightened workplace frustration (technical debt anxiety, tool-reliability concerns), which in turn reduces job satisfaction. This is a stress-buffering mediation pathway consistent with Job Demands-Resources theory (Bakker & Demerouti 2017).
mediation_hypothesis.note: Frustration is a secondary analysis; if Frustration missingness exceeds 60%, this analysis will be flagged EXPLORATORY in Stage 2.

causal_dag.description: Pre-registered causal assumptions (arrows = assumed causal direction)

paths.justification: Perceiving AI as a personal job threat induces occupational insecurity which reduces satisfaction — well-established in job insecurity literature (Cheng & Chan 2008).
Trust in AI accuracy raises the credibility of AI-as-replacement — a credible threat is psychologically more damaging than a non-credible one (Cognitive threat appraisal theory).
Threat perception activates stress responses that manifest as frustration with work conditions, which lowers satisfaction (JD-R model).
Experienced developers may feel more confident in irreplaceable skills, perceiving less AI threat — potential confound must be controlled.
Work experience correlates with seniority, autonomy, and compensation — established positive predictors of satisfaction.
Professional coding experience as proxy for specialization and market value — included as control.
Current AI users may be more aware of AI capabilities (→ higher threat) OR have integrated AI successfully (→ lower threat). Direction ambiguous — controlled but not focal.

planned_method: Moderated OLS regression (interaction model): JobSat ~ AIThreat_bin * AIAcc_ord + WorkExp + YearsCodePro + AISelect_bin

operationalizations.AIThreat_bin: Binary: 1 = Yes (perceives AI as job threat), 0 = No + I'm not sure (non-threat reference group). 'Unsure' collapsed to 0 following conservative coding — preregistered.
operationalizations.AIAcc_ord: Ordinal 1–5: Highly distrust=1, Somewhat distrust=2, Neither=3, Somewhat trust=4, Highly trust=5
operationalizations.AISelect_bin: Binary: 1 = Yes (currently uses AI tools), 0 = No/Plan to (non-user reference group)
operationalizations.Frustration_bin: Binary mediator: 1 = any frustration selected, 0 = 'None of these' — multi-select items collapsed

missing_data_strategy: Listwise deletion. Rationale: MCAR/MAR cannot be verified pre-analysis; listwise is the most transparent and conservative preregistered choice. Sensitivity analysis: multiple imputation (MICE, 5 imputations) will be reported as EXPLORATORY if sample size after listwise < 5,000.
minimum_effect_size: f² ≥ 0.02 (small; Cohen 1988) for the interaction term specifically. OR Cohen's d ≥ 0.25 for group comparisons.
power_note: With N ≈ 29,000 (JobSat non-null) and listwise deletion expected to reduce to ~10,000–15,000, power is >99% for f²=0.02 at α=0.05 — no power concern.
primary_test: Significance of interaction term β_AIThreat×AIAcc in OLS regression
secondary_tests: Simple slopes of AIThreat on JobSat at AIAcc levels 1, 3, 5 (Johnson-Neyman approach)
Baron-Kenny mediation: AIThreat → Frustration → JobSat with BCa bootstrap CIs (2000 iterations, pingouin)
BH-FDR correction across all p-values reported in Stage 2
multiverse_specifications: Spec 1 (primary): AIThreat binary × AIAcc ordinal 1–5, listwise deletion
Spec 2: AIThreat three-level (Yes/Unsure/No) as ordered factor
Spec 3: AIAcc binary (trust vs. distrust — collapse Highly+Somewhat on each side)
Spec 4: Include AISelect as additional covariate interaction with AIThreat
Spec 5: MICE-imputed dataset (5 imputations, pooled estimates)
exploratory_analyses: Does AIEthics concern (count of ethical concerns selected) predict JobSat beyond AIThreat?
Heterogeneity by DevType: Does the AIThreat × AIAcc interaction replicate within front-end, back-end, and full-stack developer subgroups?
Does AISent (overall AI sentiment) mediate or moderate the AIThreat → JobSat relationship?
Salary-controlled replication: Does the interaction hold in the subset with non-null ConvertedCompYearly (N ≈ 23,000)?
Country-level clustering: Does the interaction vary significantly across top-10 countries by respondent count?
alternative_rqs_if_primary_fails_novelty: Alt A: Does the alignment between AI capability perception (AIComplex) and actual AI adoption (AISelect) predict job satisfaction via a cognitive dissonance mechanism?
Alt B: Does overall AI ethical concern count (AIEthics items) predict job satisfaction more strongly than AI threat perception among current AI users?
reporting_plan: All preregistered analyses labeled CONFIRMATORY. All analyses not listed here labeled EXPLORATORY. Effect sizes (Cohen's f², d) and 95% BCa bootstrap CIs reported for all primary tests. CONSORT-style flow diagram for sample selection.
